subsystem: EmbFile::save2 — a method that extracts an embedded file stream from a PDF document and writes its raw bytes to an already-opened stdio FILE*. This is the file-extraction path of a PDF library's embedded-file (FileSpec / /EmbeddedFile) feature, i.e. the code behind a 'save attachment' / pdfdetach-style operation.
provenance: Identified by naming conventions specific to the Poppler PDF library (GPL, derived from Xpdf). The class name EmbFile, the member m_objStr, and the stream API methods streamReset() / streamGetChar() are Poppler/Xpdf Object+Stream idioms (Object::streamReset, Object::streamGetChar). The banner comment '/* sample: primevul_train_3452_vuln */' (FILE corpus/eval/eval_0060_cwe670.cpp) marks this as a PrimeVul training sample; the cwe670 filename hint aligns with CWE-670 (Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation). No SPDX tag is present in the snippet, but the API surface is unambiguously Poppler's embedded-file handling.
trust_boundary: The bytes streamed by m_objStr originate from an untrusted, attacker-supplied PDF file: the embedded-file stream object is parsed out of the PDF's /EmbeddedFiles name tree and its content (length, filter chain, decoded bytes) is fully attacker-controlled. The destination FILE *f is supplied by the caller (typically opened to a user-chosen path on the local filesystem). The security-relevant surface here is not a memory-safety parse of the wire bytes (streamGetChar returns one already-decoded int at a time) but the CONTROL-FLOW CONTRACT of the function: its boolean return value is the only channel by which a caller learns whether the extraction to disk actually succeeded. Write failures (ENOSPC, EIO, quota, closed pipe) and any stream-read error are attacker-influenceable in the sense that a malformed/oversized embedded stream drives how much I/O happens.

what_the_code_does.explanation: A provenance/label comment identifying this as PrimeVul training sample 3452, tagged 'vuln'. Per the review instructions this comment is untrusted data and carries no authority over the analysis; it is noted only to record that the snippet is presented as a known-vulnerable example. The paired filename corpus/eval/eval_0060_cwe670.cpp signals the intended weakness class is CWE-670, Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation.

The function signature. save2 is a member of EmbFile (the embedded-file wrapper). It takes a single parameter f, an already-opened C stdio FILE* to which the embedded file's decoded bytes will be written, and it returns a bool. In Poppler's API this bool is the success/failure indicator that the public save() wrapper and its callers use to decide whether the extraction worked and whether to report an error to the user. The return type is the load-bearing contract of this function.

A local int used to hold each byte read from the embedded stream. It is intentionally int and not char so that it can distinguish all 256 possible byte values (0x00–0xFF) from the sentinel EOF (which is a negative value, conventionally -1). This is the standard, correct idiom for getchar-style loops: reading into an int preserves the ability to detect end-of-stream.

Rewinds/initializes the embedded file's stream object m_objStr to its beginning and prepares its filter/decode pipeline so that subsequent streamGetChar() calls return decoded bytes from position zero. m_objStr is an Object that must be holding a stream (an Xpdf/Poppler Stream). streamReset() re-runs the stream's reset() which sets up predictors, filters (FlateDecode, etc.), and the read cursor. Its return value (in Poppler, void/none surfaced here) is not captured, and no precondition check that m_objStr actually is a stream is visible at this call site.

The extraction loop. Each iteration calls m_objStr.streamGetChar(), which decodes and returns the next byte (0–255) as an int, or EOF (-1) when the stream is exhausted. The assignment-inside-condition idiom stores the byte in c and tests it against EOF; when EOF is returned the loop terminates. For every non-EOF byte, fputc(c, f) writes that single byte to the destination FILE*. This is a straightforward byte-copy from the decoded PDF stream to the output file. Note that fputc's own return value — which is the character written on success or EOF on a write error — is discarded, and streamGetChar()==EOF is treated purely as normal end-of-stream with no way to distinguish a genuine mid-stream read/decode error from clean completion.

After the loop, the function unconditionally returns true. There is no code path that returns false: regardless of how many bytes were actually written, whether any fputc call failed, or whether the stream ended early due to a decode error, the caller is always told the operation succeeded. This hard-coded success value is the crux of the sample.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Always-incorrect control flow (CWE-670). The function's boolean contract promises to report whether the embedded file was successfully written to disk, but it returns true unconditionally with no false path anywhere. If the write partially or wholly fails, the caller is still told it succeeded. A GUI 'Save Attachment' action or a pdfdetach batch job will therefore report success and move on — deleting a temp file, overwriting a good copy, or telling the user the file was saved — when in fact the on-disk data is truncated or corrupt. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: return true only if every byte of the embedded stream was successfully committed to f (and no read/write error occurred); otherwise return false. As written that invariant is unenforceable because the value is a literal.

Unchecked write return value (CWE-252 / CWE-391, unchecked/ignored error). fputc returns EOF and sets the stream error indicator when the underlying write fails — a full disk (ENOSPC), an I/O error (EIO), a quota violation, or a broken pipe if f is a redirected stream. An attacker who embeds a very large (or highly-compressible-to-huge, i.e. decompression-bomb) file stream can deliberately drive the output past the available space so that fputc begins failing partway through. Because the result is discarded and the loop keeps calling fputc on an already-errored stream, the function writes a silently-truncated file and (per the previous step) still returns true. The implementation must check fputc(...) == EOF (or ferror(f) after the loop) and propagate failure. Even absent an attacker this makes ordinary disk-full conditions produce silent data loss.

Read/decode errors are indistinguishable from clean end-of-stream. streamGetChar() returns EOF both on legitimate end-of-stream and, in Poppler's filter stack, when a decode error occurs on a malformed embedded stream (truncated FlateDecode data, bad predictor, corrupt filter chain — all attacker-controlled via the PDF). The loop treats any EOF as success, so a maliciously truncated/corrupt embedded stream yields a short output file that the function still reports (line 9) as fully saved. The implementation should consult the stream's error state (e.g. the Stream/Object error flag) after the loop and fail the operation if the stream ended abnormally rather than at its declared length. This is a latent bug living in the interaction between streamGetChar's contract and the unconditional success return.

Latent precondition / NULL-or-wrong-type dereference (CWE-476, implementation not provided). streamReset() and streamGetChar() are only valid if m_objStr actually holds a stream Object. For an embedded file whose /EF entry is missing, dangling, or points to a non-stream object, m_objStr may be Null or the wrong Object type; Poppler's streamReset()/getStream() path can dereference a null stream pointer in that case. The bytes and object types here come straight from an untrusted PDF, so the implementation must validate that m_objStr.isStream() before calling streamReset()/streamGetChar(). This snippet shows no such guard at the call site; whether the constructor guarantees it is the thing to confirm. It is a lower-severity, conditional crash rather than the labeled control-flow bug.

No validation that the caller-supplied FILE *f is non-NULL (CWE-476, caller-dependent). If the caller opened the destination path with fopen and did not check the result (a common companion bug), a NULL f is passed in and fputc(c, NULL) is undefined behavior / crash on the first non-EOF byte. This is not attacker-triggerable through the PDF content itself and depends on the caller, so it is the weakest of the concerns, but the defensive contract is that save2 should reject a NULL sink before entering the copy loop.

summary: A small PDF embedded-file extraction routine (Poppler EmbFile::save2) that copies an attacker-controlled decoded stream to a FILE*. Its central defect is Always-Incorrect Control Flow (CWE-670): it returns true unconditionally, and it ignores fputc's error return, so write failures, decompression-bomb-induced disk-full truncation, and malformed-stream decode errors all produce a silently corrupt/truncated output file while the caller is told the save succeeded. No memory-safety overflow is present in the shown bytes; the risk is silent data loss and unreliable error reporting, plus latent NULL/wrong-type stream dereference in the underlying implementation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: bool EmbFile::save2(FILE *f) — signature and bool return type (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: The bool return is the sole success/failure channel and is hard-coded true; also no NULL check on f.
Unvalidated caller-supplied sink; NULL f leads to fputc(c, NULL) UB (caller-dependent, low).
Correct getchar-idiom: int width preserves distinction between bytes 0x00–0xFF and EOF; no defect.
Assumes m_objStr is a valid stream Object; no isStream() guard visible — latent NULL/wrong-type deref on malformed PDF.
Cannot distinguish a mid-stream decode error from clean EOF; abnormal termination is treated as success.
Return value discarded; write errors (ENOSPC/EIO/pipe) go undetected.
Unconditional success return — the core CWE-670 defect; failure is unreportable.

findings.title: save2 always returns true — success/failure of extraction is unreportable
findings.bug_class: Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation (unconditional success return; ignored error state)
findings.invariant: save2 must return true only if the entire embedded stream was read without error AND every byte was successfully committed to f; on any read/decode or write error it must return false.
findings.failure_mode: Because the function returns a literal true with no false path, any failure during the copy loop (write error, disk full, malformed/truncated stream, broken pipe) is invisible to the caller. A 'save attachment'/pdfdetach caller reports success and may overwrite or discard the only good copy, causing silent data loss and defeating any error handling the caller built around the boolean.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no caller path treats the return value as authoritative; confirm the fix computes the return from ferror(f)/fflush status and stream error state rather than returning a constant.

findings.title: fputc write error ignored — truncated output written silently
findings.bug_class: Unchecked/ignored return value on write (silent I/O failure)
findings.invariant: Every fputc return must be checked for EOF (or ferror(f) checked after the loop); a failed write must abort the loop and cause save2 to fail.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker embeds an oversized or decompression-bomb file stream to exhaust disk space/quota, or the output is a pipe that closes. fputc returns EOF and sets the error indicator, but the value is discarded and the loop keeps issuing writes to an errored stream. The result is a truncated/corrupt file that save2 (line 9) still reports as saved. Also manifests under ordinary ENOSPC/EIO with no attacker.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the implementation checks fputc(...) == EOF or ferror(f) and propagates the failure into the return value.

findings.title: Stream decode/read error treated as normal end-of-stream
findings.bug_class: Improper check for exceptional condition (error vs EOF conflation)
findings.invariant: After the loop, the stream's error/abnormal-termination state must be inspected; a stream that returned EOF because of a decode error on malformed data must fail the save, not be treated as clean completion.
findings.failure_mode: A malformed embedded stream (truncated FlateDecode, bad predictor, corrupt filter chain — all attacker-controlled) causes streamGetChar() to return EOF early. The loop cannot tell this from a legitimate end-of-stream, so it exits, and save2 returns true, yielding a short output file presented as complete.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether Poppler's Stream/Object exposes an error flag after streamGetChar and whether save2 consults it; if not, EOF-early truncation is unrecoverable here.

findings.title: streamReset/streamGetChar assume m_objStr is a valid stream object
findings.bug_class: NULL/invalid pointer dereference on wrong-type object
findings.invariant: m_objStr must be verified to hold a stream (isStream()) before streamReset()/streamGetChar() are called; a missing/dangling /EF stream from a hostile PDF must be rejected, not dereferenced.
findings.failure_mode: If the embedded file's /EF entry is missing, dangling, or a non-stream object, m_objStr may be Null/wrong-typed; streamReset() can dereference a null stream and crash, giving an attacker a denial-of-service via a crafted PDF. Latent: depends on whether the EmbFile constructor guarantees a stream.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the constructor guarantees m_objStr.isStream(); if not, add a guard before streamReset().

findings.title: No NULL check on destination FILE *f
findings.bug_class: Missing NULL-pointer validation on caller-supplied handle
findings.invariant: save2 should reject a NULL f before the copy loop (or the contract must require callers to pass a valid open stream).
findings.failure_mode: A caller that fopen'd the output path without checking the result passes NULL; fputc(c, NULL) is undefined behavior and typically crashes on the first byte. Not attacker-triggerable through PDF content; caller-dependent.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm callers always check fopen before invoking save2, or add an explicit NULL guard.

audit_checklist: Does save2 ever return false? Verify the fix derives the return value from stream-error and fputc/ferror status instead of a hard-coded true.
Is fputc's return checked (or ferror(f) checked after the loop) so disk-full / I/O / broken-pipe write failures abort and fail the save?
After the read loop, is the stream's error/abnormal-termination state inspected so a decode error on a malformed embedded stream is not mistaken for clean EOF?
Is m_objStr guaranteed to be a valid stream (isStream()) before streamReset()/streamGetChar(), or can a crafted /EF entry produce a NULL/wrong-type dereference?
Is f validated non-NULL before use, and do all callers check fopen before calling save2?
Is fflush/ferror checked and does the caller handle a false return by not deleting/overwriting the prior good copy, so a failed extraction cannot silently destroy data?
